text: The lady's not for turning.
context: Defiant rejection of calls to reverse her economic policies during recession.

text: You turn if you want to. The lady's not for turning.
context: The full famous line asserting resolve.

text: There is no such thing as society. There are individual men and women and there are families.
speech: Interview (often quoted as a speech line)
context: Controversial statement emphasizing personal responsibility over collective abstractions.

text: I am extraordinarily patient, provided I get my own way in the end.
context: Signature expression of her determined, uncompromising style.

text: We have become a grandmother.
speech: Statement after the birth of her first grandchild
context: Famous personal announcement that became emblematic of her public persona.

text: To those waiting with bated breath for that favorite media catchphrase, the 'U-turn,' I have only this to say: You turn if you want; the lady's not for turning.
context: The complete context of the 'not for turning' declaration.

text: Pennies do not come from heaven. They have to be earned here on earth.
source: Speech to the Scottish Conservative Conference
context: Emphasizing that prosperity must be created, not distributed from above.

text: I always cheer up immensely if an attack is particularly wounding because I think, well, if they attack one personally, it means they have not a single political argument left.
context: On distinguishing personal attacks from substantive debate.

text: If you want something said, ask a man; if you want something done, ask a woman.
context: Witty observation on gender and results.

text: Defeat? I do not recognize the meaning of the word.
source: Various (frequently quoted during the Falklands conflict)
context: Expression of absolute resolve during the Falklands campaign.

text: Europe was created by history. America was created by philosophy.
source: Speech to the American Bar Association
context: Contrasting the organic evolution of Europe with America's founding on ideas.

text: We are not in politics to ignore people's worries; we are in politics to deal with them.
context: On the duty of politicians to confront difficult realities.

text: I do not know anyone who has got to the top without hard work. That is the recipe. It will not always get you to the top, but it should get you pretty near.
context: Belief in merit, effort, and individual striving.

text: Being powerful is like being a lady. If you have to tell people you are, you aren't.
context: On the nature of real authority and presence.

text: There can be no liberty unless there is economic liberty.
context: Linking political freedom to economic freedom.

text: To cure the British disease with socialism was like trying to cure leukaemia with leeches.
source: Speech to the Conservative Party Conference
context: Critique of socialist economic policies before she became Prime Minister.

text: We must have an ideology. The other side have an ideology, they have a philosophy, they believe in it. We must have one too.
source: Speech to the Conservative Philosophy Group
context: Arguing for a clear, principled conservative alternative.

text: It is not the creation of wealth that is wrong, but the love of money for its own sake.
source: Speech to the Church of Scotland General Assembly
speech: Address to the General Assembly of the Church of Scotland
context: Defending wealth creation while acknowledging moral limits.

text: I am in politics because of the conflict between good and evil, and I believe that in the end good will triumph.
source: Various (often in interviews about her worldview)
context: Moral framing of political struggle.

text: What Britain needs is an iron lady.
source: Soviet media nickname adopted and embraced
speech: Speeches after being dubbed the 'Iron Lady'
context: She turned a Soviet insult into a badge of strength and resolve.
